detailedExemplarData.title: âTo what extent do urban stresses vary with distances from the CBD of HCMC?â

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Sharply focused, spatially explicit research question with clear theoretical framing
Three testable hypotheses directly linked to distance from the CBD
Precise geographic context (District 1, 6 km transect, 12 sites) enabling feasible primary data collection
Highâquality locator maps with scale bars, north arrows and correct conventions
weaknesses: Minor OCR errors in mathematical notation hinder clarity
Theoretical section lacks formal citations to strengthen academic rigour
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student fully meets the topâband descriptors: a clear, theoryâlinked question, focused hypotheses, specific location details and exemplary mapping. Only minor notation and citation issues prevent perfection, but the work clearly warrants full marks (3).
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Systematic transect sampling described and justified with clear relevance to the research question
Dualâobserver traffic counts and thoughtful EQS scales enhance implementation reliability
Mapping of sites in Figure 2.1 follows conventions, aiding transparency
weaknesses: Rationale for three temperature repeats is generic rather than statistically justified
Dataâcollection figures lack timestamp annotations for reproducibility
Spearmanâs rank test is mentioned without justification for its nonâparametric choice
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Methods are well described and correctly implemented, yielding reliable primary data (band 2). However, the explanation lacks comprehensiveness and theoretical alignment needed for the highest band (3), so a midârange score of 2 is appropriate.

strengths: All data sets (temperature, traffic, EQS) are directly relevant to urban stress analysis
Effective variety of presentation techniques: scatter plots, stacked columns, annotated photos
Consistent numbering of figures, labelled axes and units enhance clarity
weaknesses: Occasional axisâscale errors (e.g. 60 Â°C upper limit on a 30â36 Â°C range)
Missing legend in the stackedâcolumn chart, leading to potential confusion
Sporadic inconsistencies in reporting statistical conventions (e.g. RÂ² vs p-values)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work displays appropriate and often highly effective presentation methods, with sufficient relevant data for analysis (band 3â4). Occasional convention errors preclude full marks in this criterion but justify a solid midâtoâhigh score of 4 out of 6.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Use of descriptive statistics (means, RÂ²) and two Spearmanâs rank tests demonstrates solid analytical techniques
Anomaly discussions link field observations to data outliers effectively
Interpretation of traffic data is strengthened by integrating bidârent theory
Spearmanâs rank result (râ=0.7, 95% confidence) is well executed, supporting Hypothesis 2
weaknesses: RÂ² values are sometimes misinterpreted as correlation strength rather than variance explained
Lack of p-values or confidence intervals for some tests reduces statistical rigour
Hypothesis 1 and 3 analyses remain partly descriptive; quantitative support is incomplete
Explanations occasionally speculative without sufficient evidence depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis demonstrates strong descriptive and inferential techniques, links to theory and thorough anomaly interpretation. However, misinterpretation of statistics and gaps in quantitative testing prevent full marks in the top bands. A score of 5 balances these strengths and weaknesses within an 8-point scale.

strengths: Conclusion revisits each hypothesis and succinctly summarises outcomes
Statements are supported by evidence drawn from the analysis
weaknesses: Synthesis could more explicitly tie findings back to the central research question
Depth of reflection on broader implications and variable interplay is limited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clear, fully supported by the preceding analysis and meets the descriptor for full marks. Minor depth issues prevent further elaboration, so a score of 2 out of 2 is justified.

strengths: Thoughtful reflection on systematic sampling and repeat measurements
Precise discussion of timeâof-day limitations and their impact on data quality
Relevant suggestions for increasing sample size and piloting the EQS scale
weaknesses: Improvement suggestions lack detailed plans for implementation or redesign
Deeper discussion of spatial representativeness and clustering bias is needed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation appropriately outlines key strengths and weaknesses with relevant improvement suggestions (band 2). It lacks the comprehensive depth and detailed impact analysis required for band 3, warranting a score of 2 out of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two well-constructed locator maps are provided: a national/regional inset with scale & north arrow and a detailed city-scale map plotting every site along the transect.  Both observe cartographic conventions (scale bars, orientation arrow, key).  These satisfy the âclear locational maps following conventionsâ requirement of the top band.

The student poses a sharply-focused geographical question that is explicitly spatial (âurban stresses vary with distance from the CBDâ) and immediately rooted in syllabus Option G.  Three testable hypotheses are stated, demonstrating that the question naturally lends itself to hypothesis formation and statistical testing.  Clear links are drawn to urban heat-island and bid-rent theory, showing explicit theoretical framing.  This matches the descriptor for the top band.

A rich variety of visualisations is used: locator maps, scatter diagrams with trendlines, stacked columns, annotated photographs and Spearmanâs significance graph.  These generally enhance understanding, so the work sits solidly in the âappropriate techniquesâ zone and edges toward âmost effectiveâ; a score of 4 within band 3-4 is justified.

Data were gathered at 12 sites with repeated readings and dual-observer traffic counts, which are correctly implemented.  Yet the sample remains small and restricted to one two-hour period; potential biases (weekday choice, micro-site relocation) are not mitigated.  Reliability is acceptable but not of the âhigh-qualityâ standard demanded for band 3.

The student calculates means, RÂ² values and two Spearmanâs rank coefficients, demonstrating appropriate descriptive and inferential techniques.  Some statistical interpretation is weak (RÂ² portrayed as correlation strength) and confidence levels are only partly discussed, preventing entry to the 5-6 band.  Therefore 4 (upper band 3-4) is appropriate.

Trends and anomalies are identified for each variable and linked back to urban models, but explanations are sometimes superficial or speculative (e.g., vegetation patterns âuniqueâ without evidence).  Links to methods/theory exist yet are not comprehensive.  This matches the mid-band descriptor.

The CBD is pinpointed as District 1 of Ho Chi Minh City and a 6 km west-bound transect is described with 12 sites at 500 m intervals.  The student justifies the direction and demonstrates feasibility (single morning, handheld instruments).  The context paragraph supplies population data and a regional map, so the geographic setting is specific, relevant and clearly conducive to primary data collection.

The evaluation lists several pertinent strengths (systematic sampling) and weaknesses (time of day, small sample, subjective EQS).  Explanations show why these matter but lack the exhaustive depth expected for band 3.  Thus band 2 is appropriate.

All collected data sets (temperature, traffic, EQS) are clearly relevant to measuring urban stress.  The 12-site transect supplies enough points for basic correlation tests, but the write-up itself notes that the sample size limits validity.  This meets the âmost data relevant allowing partial analysisâ descriptor, hence low end of band 3-4.

The conclusion revisits each hypothesis, summarises outcomes and references supporting evidence, albeit briefly.  It is clearly derived from the preceding analysis, so the higher band is reached; however, depth of synthesis is limited, so the full-marks descriptor is not met.

All three hypotheses are addressed, but analysis for temperature and residential quality is acknowledged as inconclusive.  Gaps remain (no quantitative test for hypothesis 1).  The strand therefore fulfils âanalysis with some gapsâ, warranting 3 in band 3-4.

Methods for temperature, traffic and EQS are described, with instruments, sampling interval and timing explained, and limited discussion of why each variable represents urban stress.  However, links to underlying theory (e.g., why Spearmanâs rank is appropriate) are only briefly mentioned and the rationale for three repeats is generic.  Detail is adequate but not âcomprehensive,â fitting band 2.

Figures are numbered, axes labelled and units shown, but there are occasional lapses (e.g., temperature graph y-axis mis-titled 60 Â°C upper limit, inconsistent RÂ² explanation).  Such sporadic errors align with âoccasional errors in conventionsâ, placing the strand in the middle of band 3-4.

Improvement ideasâalter sampling time, pilot EQS, enlarge sample sizeâare relevant and their likely impact is briefly explained.  While valid, the discussion is not detailed enough (no specific redesign of methods) for the top band, so it earns band 2.

annotations.comment: Hypotheses 2 and 3 are testable and clearly linked to distance from the CBD, fulfilling requirements for hypothesis formation.

annotations.comment: The y-axis upper limit in Figure 3.1 is set at 60 Â°C but data range is 30â36 Â°C. Adjust axes to improve readability.

annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The link to Option G âUrban Environmentsâ is well explained, demonstrating clear alignment with the IB syllabus and theory.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The overview of urban heat island and bidârent models is apt, but citations are absent. Include specific references to strengthen theoretical links.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The systematicâsampling transect is well described and justified, showing clear relevance to the research question.
Figure 2.1 follows mapping conventions for the transect and sites, supporting method transparency.
The rationale for three temperature repeats is generic. Explain why three readings suffice statistically or consider calculating measurement error.
Dualâobserver traffic counts increase reliability and reduce bias, demonstrating highâquality implementation.
Figures 2.3â2.4 effectively document data collection but lack timestamp annotation. Include exact times to support reproducibility.
The EQS methodology uses clear scales to minimize bias, demonstrating thoughtful qualitative method design.
Spearmanâs rank is mentioned but not justified. Explain why a nonâparametric test is appropriate given data characteristics.
The analysis of Hypothesis 1 misinterprets trendline direction: a positive slope contradicts the predicted decrease. Clarify correlation sign.
Using a bar graph to explore multiple variables is valid, but deepen analysis by computing correlation coefficients for temperature vs shade and vegetation.
Anomaly discussion for sites 10â11 is strong qualitative insight, linking environment features to data outliers.
When interpreting RÂ² in Hypothesis 2, avoid equating it directly with significance. Report p-values or confidence intervals.
The use of Spearmanâs rank (râ=0.7) with 95% confidence is well executed, providing inferential support for Hypothesis 2.
The integration of bid-rent theory to explain traffic patterns strengthens the link between data interpretation and geographic theory.
Hypothesis 3 analysis is descriptive but lacks quantitative testing. Consider running Spearmanâs rank for EQS vs distance.
Stacked column chart (Figure 3.8) is appropriate, but color key is missing. Add legend to clarify variable identification.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Anomaly explanation for Site 4 uses field observation effectively to contextualize EQS data.
The conclusion summarizes each hypothesis outcome but could more explicitly tie findings back to the central research question.
While the conclusion identifies complex factor interplay, synthesizing patterns across all variables would enhance coherence.
The evaluation reflects on systematic sampling and trial repeats, showing thoughtful reflection on methodological strengths.
The discussion of timeâofâday limitations is precise and relevant, with clear suggestions for richer data capture at peak hours.
The proposed pilot testing of the EQS scales is logical; expand to detail how feedback would refine the instrument before field deployment.
Sampleâsize limitation is well identified; further discuss spatial representativeness and potential clustering bias for deeper evaluation.
